text: The laparoscopic view reveals a large anterior myoma on the anterior wall of the uterus, multiple small subserous myomas, and a large pedunculated myoma attached to the posterior wall of the uterus.
The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator to perform uterine inversion, revealing multiple additional myomas on the posterior wall of the uterus.
A myoma is being pulled from the right iliac fossa towards the upper abdomen, revealing the pedicle attachment of the myoma.
The surgeon injects vasopressin into the surgical field to induce transient ischemia, reducing blood flow to the area surrounding the myomas.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the pedicle of a large pedunculated myoma attached to the posterior wall of the uterus, with the surgical field dilated using twenty ml of saline.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device, which vibrates at fifteen thousand times per second, to cut through the tissue.
The surgeon sets the harmonic device to minimum power and slowly dissects two myomas from their attachment to the uterus. One of the myomas is notably large.
Two subserous pedunculated myomas are visible, one large and one small, attached to the uterus.
The surgeon detaches a larger myoma along with a smaller one from the uterus. A bipolar device is applied to the base of a third myoma for removal.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device to cut the pedicle of a pedunculated myoma attached to the posterior wall of the uterus, completing the detachment process.
The removed myomas are deposited into the cul-de-sac, with the surgeon keeping count of the number of excised myomas to ensure accurate tracking.
The surgeon injects vasopressin into the anterior wall myoma, preparing for further dissection or removal.
A jet of vasopressin is visibly emerging from a puncture site on the uterine surface.
The transient ischemia causes the myoma to become more palpable, enhancing its visibility for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the anterior wall of the serous layer of the myoma, demonstrating precise tissue separation.

After splitting the serous layer, the surgeon introduces a myoma screw and applies anterosuperior traction to the myoma. The myoma is slowly enucleated, revealing the ease of removal when a good plane is established.
The surgeon applies slow traction to the myoma, gradually separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the fourth myoma within the surgical field, preparing for its enucleation.
The surgeon completes the separation of the myoma and places it into the cul-de-sac, indicating the myoma has been fully enucleated from its surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the fifth myoma, which is pedunculated and subsiding, making it easy to remove from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon identifies the sixth myoma, preparing for its removal.
The surgeon completes the separation of the myoma and performs suction irrigation to clear the surgical field.
The surgeon sutures the anterior wall of the myoma, closing the incision site with precise needle placement and thread manipulation.
The surgeon uses a Y-Grill instrument and a 30 mm needle to take a deep bite, securing the tissue with a tumble square knot.
The surgeon ties a single half knot during the suturing process on the anterior wall of the myoma, using a Y-Grill and a 30 mm needle.

The surgeon performs a square knot with the needle end kept straight, forming a reverse C shape. The second half of the knot is taken but kept loose, and the needle end is straightened in a technique known as tumbling.

The surgeon uses the tip of a Maryland dissector to slide and tighten the suture, ensuring firm approximation of the tissue to prevent bleeding. The initial tumble square knot is followed by continuous suturing to secure the area.
The surgeon secures the suture with an additional lock, ensuring the knot is tightened to maintain tissue approximation.
The surgeon ties a temple square knot, followed by an additional lock to secure the suture, ensuring the knot is tight and stable.
The surgeon completes another lock in the suture and instructs the assistant to hold the suture in place.
The surgeon takes a further bite with the needle, continuing the suturing process on the tissue.

The surgeon carefully interlocks sutures within the 'C' shape of the incision to ensure proper approximation of the uterine wall around the subserous myoma, preventing weakening of the uterine structure.
The surgeon takes another deep bite with the suture needle into the uterine tissue, ensuring secure approximation of the layers during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon ties a knot to control bleeding from a highly vascular area, tightening and locking the suture with a surgeon's knot to ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon performs ipsilateral suturing, locking the suture in place. The manipulation angle is visibly less than sixty degrees, indicating the increased difficulty compared to contralateral suturing.
The surgeon secures the final lock on the suture, completing the knot tying process during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses suction irrigation to clear the surgical field, revealing the serosal folds.
The surgeon takes an additional suture bite to control bleeding in the surgical field, ensuring hemostasis.
The surgeon tightens a tumbled square knot using a Maryland dissector, initially keeping the knot loose before pushing it into place.
The surgeon identifies and separates a myoma located in the posterior wall of the uterus, marking it as the seventh myoma encountered during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully pulls out the myoma using a myoma screw, emphasizing the importance of maintaining a good dissection plane. Blunt dissection is performed to separate the myoma from surrounding tissue. The posterior wall of the uterus is sutured, ensuring that the suture bites do not extend to the fallopian tube.
An instrument is used to lift the uterus, providing better access and visibility for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon performs suturing and knotting on the uterine wall following the removal of the seventh myoma from the posterior wall.
The surgeon ties a resurgence knot during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector, with one hook leg visibly projected, indicating it will be adjusted later.
The surgeon takes a second suture bite on the uterine tissue, with visible bleeding that is expected to stop immediately after the suture is placed.
The surgeon carefully guards the suture while pulling it through the loop, ensuring it is securely locked in place.
The surgeon places and tightens knots at the appropriate locations, followed by further locking of the sutures. Minor bleeding is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon discusses the option to use electrocautery to control bleeding, emphasizing the need to avoid excessive use of the current.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector with a projection to hold the tissue, which is then further tightened.
The surgeon tightens the suture and cuts it, followed by thorough suction and irrigation to clear the surgical field.
The uterus is shown without any visible myoma, and the surgeon trims the suture. The surgical field is clear of bleeding.
The surgeon performs thorough suction and irrigation within the uterine cavity, ensuring the surgical field is clear of fluids and debris.

The surgeon begins the process of morselation, carefully using the morselator to gradually remove the large myoma. One ear of the morselator remains visible during the procedure, ensuring precise control and visibility as the myoma is slowly morselated out.
